title: Dog Bunny Hopping While Running: Causes and Triage Guide
meta_desc: Is your dog bunny hopping while running? Learn about hip dysplasia, gait changes, and when to see a vet in this empathetic triage guide.
content_html:
What is Bunny Hopping?

Bunny hopping is a specific gait where a dog moves both hind legs together simultaneously, rather than in an alternating pattern, while running or climbing stairs. While it might look cute or quirky, it is often a compensatory movement used by dogs to minimize discomfort or instability in their hips or lower back. By moving both legs together, they shift the weight-bearing burden and reduce the range of motion required in individual joints.

Is it an Emergency?

The urgency level for bunny hopping is generally Low. If your dog is otherwise eating, drinking, and able to walk, this is not typically an immediate medical emergency. However, it is a clinical sign that should not be ignored. It is usually indicative of a chronic orthopedic condition, such as hip dysplasia or luxating patellas, and warrants a scheduled appointment with your veterinarian for a physical exam and potential X-rays.

Why Video and Photos Help Triage

Capturing a video of your dog running in their natural environment is one of the most helpful tools you can provide a veterinary professional. Dogs often experience an adrenaline rush at the clinic that can temporarily mask lameness or gait abnormalities. A clear video—especially one taken at eye level from the side and from behind—allows your vet to see the exact mechanics of the hop, helping them differentiate between hip, knee, or spinal issues before the physical exam even begins.

faqs.question: Does bunny hopping always mean hip dysplasia?
faqs.answer: While bunny hopping is a classic sign of hip dysplasia, it can also be caused by patellar luxation, spinal issues, or even temporary muscle strain in younger dogs.

faqs.question: At what age do dogs start bunny hopping?
faqs.answer: It can be seen in puppies as young as a few months old if they have congenital hip issues, but it may also develop later in life as arthritis or joint degredation progresses.

faqs.question: Should I limit my dog's exercise if I see them bunny hopping?
faqs.answer: It is best to avoid high-impact activities like jumping or long-distance running until a veterinarian has evaluated the cause, as excessive strain can worsen joint laxity.
